era_context: Egypt's Old Kingdom, Dynasty IV, under Khafre (or possibly his successor Menkaure, depending on chronology). Akkadian Empire under Sargon of Akkad in Mesopotamia. This period represents the continuation of Old Kingdom monumental culture and Akkadian imperial consolidation.

events.title: Possible transition in Egyptian Dynasty IV leadership
events.description: Depending on the chronology adopted, 2536 BCE may fall near the end of Khafre's reign or the beginning of his successor Menkaure's reign. The transition between these pharaohs is not precisely dated. Menkaure (c. 2532–2503 BCE under conventional chronology) commissioned the Pyramid of Menkaure at Giza, the smallest of the three main pyramids. The exact year of succession is uncertain, but it likely occurred within a few years of this date.
events.certainty_note: The transition between Khafre and Menkaure is attested in king lists and archaeology, but the precise year is uncertain. Estimates vary by ±5–10 years depending on the chronological system used.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the transition between Khafre and Menkaure is uncertain. Some scholars place Menkaure's reign earlier (c. 2548–2520 BCE), which would move the transition to before 2536 BCE. Others place it later, which would move the transition to after 2536 BCE. The chronological uncertainty is ±10–20 years.
historiographic_note: The record for 2536 BCE is extremely sparse. No contemporary sources explicitly date to this year. The only information available is inferred from king lists and archaeology. The transition between pharaohs is a significant political event, but the exact timing cannot be determined from surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of Dynasty IV rule in Egypt.
